coredata.dc:title: In-situ synthesis of Cu2OAu nanocomposites as nanozyme for colorimetric determination of hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Daxiang
Liu, Hongying
Ma, Huan
Huang, Zhiheng
Gu, Chunchuan
Zhang, Mingzhen
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, gold nanoparticles-doped Cu2O cubes (Cu2OAu nanocomposites) with peroxide-like character were readily prepared through a simple two-step method and were used for sensitive readout of H2O2 with color variation for the first time. By changing reaction temperature and feed rate, Cu2O nanocrystals with very small size were obtained and were then used to prepare Cu2OAu nanocomposites. HR-TEM, SEM, and XRD were used to characterize their morphology and size. The results revealed that the as-prepared Cu2OAu nanocomposites displayed rough cube morphology, with Cu2O cube, ca. 85 nm in size, filled with 8 nm spheres. In the presence of H2O2, 3,3,5′,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) was catalyzed by Cu2OAu nanocomposites and generated color changes, which was employed to detect H2O2. By comparison with Cu2O nanocrystals with different sizes, Cu2OAu nanocomposites exhibited highest peroxide-like activity, which relies heavily on the pH and temperature in the reaction systems and satisfies with the Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Based on these findings, a colorimetric method was developed to detect H2O2 over a range between 0.4 μM and 1000 μM. This platform extends a great prospect in the field of clinical diagnosis, food safety, and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu2OAu nanocomposites
Hydrogen peroxide
Colorimetric method
Biosensor
Peroxides-like

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Cu2O nanocrystals and Cu2OAu nanocomposites
Study on the peroxide-like activity of Cu2OAu
The stability experiment of Cu2OAu nanocomposites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology Program of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financed by the NSFC ( 21405029 and 51173035 ), Science and Technology Program of Zhejiang Province ( LGF18H200005 ), Young Talent Development Project of Zhejiang Science and Technology Association ( 2016YCGC007 ), and the Social Development Project of Hangzhou ( 20160533B70 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of Cu2O nanocrystals prepared at various conditions. (A and B) Cu2O nanocrystals were prepared with slow addition of AA at 55 °C and 43 °C, respectively. (C and D) Cu2O nanocrystals were prepared with quick addition of AA at 43 °C and 25 °C, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy of Cu2OAu nanocomposites (A and B). TEM of Cu2OAu nanocomposites (C). HR-TEM of Cu2OAu nanocomposites (D). EDX of Cu2OAu nanocomposites (E). XRD patterns of Cu2OAu nanocomposites and Cu2O nanocrystals (F).

(A) The relative absorbance of the oxTMB at 652 nm produced in various systems: (a) H2O2 Cu2OAu-TMB, (b) TMB-H2O2, and (c) Cu2OAu-TMB in acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.0) at 37 °C. (B) Relative catalytic activity of Cu2O with prepared at different temperatures, Cu2OAu and AuNPs: (a) Cu2O prepared at 55 °C. (b) Cu2O prepared at 43 °C. (c) Cu2O prepared with at 25 °C. (d) Cu2OAu prepared at 25 °C. (e) AuNPs.

(A) Relative catalytic activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites incubated in acetic acid buffer (0.1 M) with various pH values at 37 °C for 30 min. (B) Relative catalytic activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites incubated in acetic acid buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.0) with various temperatures for 30 min.

Kinetic analysis assay for Cu2OAu nanocomposites: (A) fixed substrate was TMB (0.8 mM), the concentration of H2O2 is variable. (B) H2O2 substrate was fixed at 1.2 mM whereas the concentration of TMB was changed. (C and D) Double reciprocal curves of activity for Cu2OAu nanocomposites with different concentrations of fixed substrate. Experimental conditions: reaction in acetic acid buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.0) for 30 min at 45 °C.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of TMB in the present of various concentrations of H2O2 using Cu2OAu nanocomposites as enzyme. (B) The linear relationship between concentrations of H2O2 and absorbance of oxTMB using Cu2OAu nanocomposites as enzyme. Inset: photographs of Cu2OAu-TMB system after the reaction with different concentrations of H2O2.
Reaction mechanism of H2O2 Cu2OAu-TMB systems.
Kinetic parameters of Cu2OAu nanocomposites and HRP.

head.ce:title: In-situ synthesis of Cu2OAu nanocomposites as nanozyme for colorimetric determination of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Information Science & Instrument Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Information Science & Instrument Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Medical Information and 3D Bioprinting of Zhejiang Province, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Medical Information and 3D Bioprinting of Zhejiang Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Hangzhou Cancer Hospital, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310002, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, gold nanoparticles-doped Cu2O cubes (Cu2OAu nanocomposites) with peroxide-like character were readily prepared through a simple two-step method and were used for sensitive readout of H2O2 with color variation for the first time. By changing reaction temperature and feed rate, Cu2O nanocrystals with very small size were obtained and were then used to prepare Cu2OAu nanocomposites. HR-TEM, SEM, and XRD were used to characterize their morphology and size. The results revealed that the as-prepared Cu2OAu nanocomposites displayed rough cube morphology, with Cu2O cube, ca. 85 nm in size, filled with 8 nm spheres. In the presence of H2O2, 3,3,5′,5′-tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) was catalyzed by Cu2OAu nanocomposites and generated color changes, which was employed to detect H2O2. By comparison with Cu2O nanocrystals with different sizes, Cu2OAu nanocomposites exhibited highest peroxide-like activity, which relies heavily on the pH and temperature in the reaction systems and satisfies with the Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Based on these findings, a colorimetric method was developed to detect H2O2 over a range between 0.4 μM and 1000 μM. This platform extends a great prospect in the field of clinical diagnosis, food safety, and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Accurate, sensitive and fast detection of H2O2 is of critical importance in the field of clinical diagnosis, food safety, industrial, and pharmaceutical as well as environmental monitoring. Enormous efforts have been made to develop some new methods based on spectrometry [1], chemiluminescence [2], and electrochemistry [3]. Recently, significant attention has been attracted to expand colorimetric method due to low cost, high sensitivity, simplified process, and even convenience to be read out by unaided eyes [4,5]. In particular, nanozyme-based colorimetric sensors have opened a new door and continues to boom because of their low cost, high stability and super catalytic activity [6].

Since Fe3O4 was first reported as one of nanozyme systems, various nanostructure materials including noble metal nanostructures [7], metal oxide nanostructures [8], carbon nanostructures [9], and layered double hydroxides nanosheets [10] have demonstrated excellent peroxides-like catalytic activities. By comparison, metal oxide nanomaterials have attracted enormous interest due to their low cost, highly specific surface area, controllable synthesis in large scale, and the lattice matching is easy to implement. Up to date, various metal oxides, such as CeO2 [11], Co3O4 nanoparticles [12], V2O5 nanowires [13], and CuO nanoclusters [6] have been used to fabricate colorimetric H2O2 sensors by making full use of peroxides-like activity.

As a p-type semiconductor, cuprous oxide (Cu2O) has been widely used in the field of solar cell, catalysis, and sensor in consideration of its unique properties including low cost, large-scale production, controllable structure as well as morphological multiformity [14]. Significant efforts have been endeavored to synthesize Cu2O-based nanomaterials with several morphologies including cubes, spheres, octahedral as well as rhombic dodecahedra [15–19]. However, their applications were limited due to the low stability of Cu2O. To tackle this problem, noble metals or alloy nanoparticles were employed [20–28] to modify the Cu2O nanomaterials. Up to now, most of them applied in the field of electrochemical biosensors have demonstrated to improve sensitivity and wide linear detection. To our knowledge, there has been no study which investigates their peroxides-like catalytic properties for the fabrication of colorimetric sensors.

In this study, Cu2OAu nanocomposites were readily synthesized via a facile two-step method and then were used as a probe for the quantitative detection of H2O2 by taking advantage of their peroxides-like properties for the first time. To that end, Cu2OAu nanocomposites with small size and narrow particle size distribution were first prepared, during which experimental conditions were optimized. Next, HR-TEM, SEM, and XRD were used to characterize their morphology, size, and composition. Finally, the peroxide-like activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites was evaluated. Herein, a new colorimetric method was developed for the quantitative detection of H2O2, which took advantage of the peroxides-like properties of the Cu2OAu nanocomposites.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), l-ascorbic acid (AA), and gold chloride hydrate (HAuCl4) were gained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). CuCl2·2H2O (99%), NaOH (97%), and sodium citrate dehydrate (99%) were received from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (China). TMB (99%) was obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (China). The rest of the chemicals employed were of analytical reagent grade and all aqueous solutions were freshly prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 ΜΩ). 0.1 M acetic acid buffer with a pH value ranged from 2 to 12 were prepared with different proportions of 0.1 M HAc and 0.1 M NaAc.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN (FEI, USA) electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Images of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were acquired using a JSM-7500 field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) with an EDX spectrometer. Samples for SEM or TEM were prepared by dropping the dispersed solution on an ITO slide which placed on a copper disc or a copper film respectively, and then drying in air. XRD spectrum was acquired using a BRUKER D8 with Cu rotating anode target at voltage of 18 kV. Ultraviolet absorption experiments were analyzed on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The relatively absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm was measured with a Varioskan Flash (Thermo fisher Scientific, USA).

A facile two-step method was used to prepare Cu2OAu nanocomposites. The first step was to synthesize Cu2O nanocrystals according to the literature [29]. Particularly, 10 ml of 1 M NaOH aqueous solution was added dropwise to 50 ml of 0.01 M CuCl2 aqueous solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Subsequently, 5 ml of 0.6 M ascorbic acid was mixed with the above solution under continuous stirring for 60 min and the whole process was carried out in a pure nitrogen condition. The products were centrifuged and rinsed three times by ultrapure water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, they were dried in vacuum at room temperature for 12 h.

Next, Cu2OAu nanocomposites were prepared by in-situ synthesis of Au NPs on Cu2O nanocrystals surface. 2 ml of 1 wt% sodium citrate was added to 2 ml of 12.5 mg ml−1 Cu2O nanocrystals and ultrasonic dispersion for 10 min. Subsequently, the above mixture was added quickly into 100 ml of 0.01 wt% gold chloride hydrate aqueous solution to form a dark green turbid liquid under vigorous stirring for 15 min. The obtained products were centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, the products were dried in vacuum at room temperature for 12 h.

The peroxides-like activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites was determined by investigating the catalytic oxidation of the peroxides substrate in the presence of H2O2. Experiments were carried out as follows: 80 μl of 1 mg ml−1 Cu2OAu nanocomposites, 50 μl TMB (40 mM) and 50 μl H2O2 (1.0 M) was dropped into 1820 μl of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4), respectively. Then, the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, the peroxide-like activity was evaluated by recording UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy at 652 nm. In order to achieve maximum catalytic effect, some parameters were optimized separately as follows: temperature adjustment between 30 and 60 °C and pH adjustment between 2 and 12.

We carried out steady-state kinetics experiments to elucidate the catalytic reaction mechanism of Cu2OAu nanocomposites. Specifically, 80 μl of 1 mg ml−1 Cu2OAu nanocomposites was added to 1920 μl of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4) containing 50 μl different consistency of TMB and regular consistency of H2O2, or vice versa. The reaction took place at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm was measured, which was employed to calculate the concentration of oxTMB according to the Beer-Lambert Law [30]:
A = Klc
A is absorbance, K is molar absorption coefficient (Lmol−1cm−1), l is optical path (cm), c is molar concentration (M). Double reciprocal curve was employed to measure the kinetic parameters in accordance with the Michaelis-Menten equation [31]:
V = V max × [S]/(K
m + [S])
where V is the initial velocity (M s−1), V max stands for the maximal reaction velocity (M s−1), [S] stands for substrate consistency (M), and K
m stands for the Michaelis-Menten constant (M).

The quantitative detection of H2O2 was carried out as described below: 80 μl of 1 mg ml−1 Cu2OAu nanocomposites was added to 1920 μl of acetic acid buffer (0.1 M, pH = 4) containing 50 μl of various consistency of H2O2 and 50 μl of 20 mM TMB, followed by incubation at 45 °C for 30 min before UV absorption measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Cu2O nanocrystals and Cu2OAu nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2O nanocrystals are the cornerstones of Cu2OAu nanocomposites. Thus, Cu2O nanocrystals with uniform morphology were achieved by optimizing process parameters, including the reaction temperature and feed rate conditions. FESEM was used to investigate the morphology evolution of Cu2O nanocrystals at different conditions. Fig. 1 displays the FESEM images of Cu2O nanocrystals prepared at various reaction temperatures and feed rate conditions. The size of Cu2O nanocrystals in Fig. 1A–D was ∼950 nm, ∼226 nm, ∼175 nm, and ∼80 nm, respectively. In addition, the resulting Cu2O nanocrystals also exhibited narrow size distribution, as evidenced from Fig. 1. These results demonstrated that our method is advantageous in the synthesis of Cu2O nanocrystals.

In order to gain insight into how reaction conditions (i.e., temperature and feed rate) affect the resulting Cu2O nanocrystals, a study of reaction temperature on the size of Cu2O was conducted first. Obviously, the size of Cu2O nanocrystals gradually decreased with decreasing reaction temperature due to crystal nucleation and growth directly related to temperature [32], as shown in Fig. 1A and B. As the temperature was decreased from 55 °C to 25 °C, the nucleation rate of crystal was faster than the growth rate resulting in decreased crystal size. Subsequently, the feed rate condition was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1C and D, with the accelerated injection of AA, the diameter of Cu2O nanocrystals decreased and the size distribution became homogeneous. The reason was that rapid feed rate increases the nucleation rate of the crystal, and then contributes to the uniform growth of the crystal. Hence, Cu2O nanocrystals with quickly dripping AA at 25 °C were selected for future preparation of Cu2OAu nanocomposites.

Next, Cu2OAu nanocomposites were prepared by in-situ synthesis of AuNPs on Cu2O nanocrystals surface, and were characterized by FESEM, TEM, EDX, and XRD. A typical SEM for Cu2OAu nanocomposites revealed the formation of a cubic structure with rough surface and with an average size of 85 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 A and B. Spherical particles of 8 nm are observed to be attached densely and uniformly on the surface of Cu2O nanocrystals, as evidenced from the TEM images showed in Fig. 2C and D. Moreover, the lattice spacing of 0.214 nm and 0.204 nm in the HR-TEM micrographs (Fig. 2D) correspond to the (200) planes of Cu2O and the (200) planes of Au, respectively, indicating the coating of AuNPs on the surface of Cu2O nanocrystals.

To further study the internal composition and structure of Cu2OAu nanocomposites, EDX as well as XRD was performed and mapped (Fig. 2E and F). EDX results revealed the presence of Cu, O, Au elements in the sample. Meanwhile, all standard diffraction peaks of Cu2O nanocrystals appeared in the XRD pattern of Cu2OAu nanocomposites (JCPDS 05–0667), where the largest peak at 2θ values of 36.4° corresponds to (111) planes, and the diffraction curves at 29.6°, 42.3°, 61.3°, 73.5°, 77.3° represent (110), (200), (220), (311), (222) planes of Cu2O crystals, respectively. In addition, three diffraction curves of 38.2°, 44.4° and 64.6° also appeared, which correspond to the (111), (200) and (220) planes of Au (JCPDS 04–0784), respectively. However, the intensity of the diffraction peaks of Cu2OAu nanocomposites is relatively weaker than that of Cu2O nanocrystals (Fig. 2F). This is because part of Cu+ participated in the reaction and then destroyed external structure of crystal when Au fixing on the surface of Cu2O nanocrystals. The reaction equation is described as follows [33]:
3 C
u 2
O + 2 A u C
l 4 −
+ 6
H +
= 6 C
u
2 +
+ 2 A u + 3
H 2
O + 8 C
l −
The above results indicated that Au nanoparticles are uniformly immobilized on the surface of Cu2O nanocrystals to form rough surface of cubic particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the peroxide-like activity of Cu2OAu
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, catalysts based on metal oxides have been widely used in the detection of substances because of their good optical and chemical properties [34–37]. It has been reported that Cu2OAu nanocomposites can electrochemical detect H2O2 [33]. However, there have been no reports of peroxide-like properties for Cu2OAu nanocomposites. Herein, Cu2OAu nanocomposites were used to catalyze oxidation of TMB to produce color reaction in the presence of H2O2 to verify their enzymatic activity. As shown in Fig. 3 A, with the existence of H2O2, a significant blue reaction and a biggest absorption at 652 nm was observed after the addition of Cu2OAu nanocomposites. Under same condition, colorless solution and no absorbance at 652 nm was obtained for Cu2OAuTMB reaction system. It indicated that the two components are independent in the system. Moreover, the absorbance at wavelength of 652 nm for Cu2OAuTMBH2O2 system is much higher than that of TMB-H2O2 system, demonstrating excellent peroxide-like catalytic property of Cu2OAu nanocomposites. The reason is that Cu+ catalyzes H2O2 to produce reactive oxygen species which can oxidize TMB quickly [38], as shown in Scheme 1 . Subsequently, the influence of particle size of Cu2O nanocrystals on its catalytic activity was figured out in Fig. 3B. The catalytic activities of Cu2O nanocrystals gradually increased with decreasing the size due to their large particular surface area. Furthermore, the activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites compared with Cu2O nanocrystals and AuNPs, respectively. The result revealed that the catalytic activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites overmatchs that of Cu2O nanocrystals and AuNPs due to the following two main reasons: first, excellent synergistic effect between Cu2O nanocrystals and AuNPs. When AuNPs are deposited on the cuprous oxide surface, it results in increased active sites on the cuprous oxide surface, contributing to better combination between the Cu2OAu nanocomposites and the substrate. Second, decoration of AuNPs on Cu2O nanocrystals hampers the oxidation of Cu2O nanocrystals.

To achieve best catalytic activity, pH and temperature of the reaction system were optimized and are displayed in Fig. 4 . The results showed that the absorbance at 652 nm, which is ascribed to oxTMB, increased gradually with elevating pH and started to drop off after pH 4. In the case of temperature, the trend was similar to that obtained from the pH optimization experiment. Maximum activity was observed at 45 °C. These results verified that pH and temperature have significant effects on the activity of reaction system, therefore, the optimal reaction conditions are as follows: pH is 4 and temperature is 45 °C.

In order to further explore the catalysis mechanism of Cu2OAu nanocomposites, dynamic analyses of Cu2OAu nanocomposites were carried out in a steady state with TMB and H2O2 as substrates. Fig. 5 shows the Michaelis–Menten curves and double reciprocal curves by fixing concentration of TMB and changing the concentration of H2O2 as well as fixing concentration of H2O2 and changing the concentration of TMB, respectively. Km and V max were first calculated through double reciprocal Michaelis–Menten plots and are summarized in Table 1 .

Comparison of dynamics parameters revealed that the Km value of Cu2OAu nanocomposites with H2O2 as the fixed substrate is 6.9 times smaller than that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP). It indicated that the affinity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites with the TMB is higher than that of HRP, resulting in a higher catalytic activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites. Such property was benefited from the nano-size and large specific surface area of Cu2OAu nanocomposites as well as the combination of Au nanoparticles on the surface of Cu2O nanocrystals and amide groups of TMB molecule by AuN bonding [40]. Moreover, several double reciprocal lines were obtained by changing the concentration range of TMB and H2O2 substrate as shown in Fig. 5C and D, respectively. Data showed that the lines are parallel to each other, corresponding to the ping-pong mechanism [41]. It means Cu2OAu nanocomposites binds and reacts with the first basement, and subsequently releases the outcome prior to responding to the second basement. There was no third party medium of an enzyme throughout the process.

The above experimental results have proved that the solution color and absorbance at 652 nm of TMB-H2O2 system changed with the addition of Cu2OAu nanocomposites. The depth of color and the absorbance value is related to the concentration of H2O2. Therefore, an easy colorimetric way for identification of H2O2 was devised.

Under optimal reaction conditions, the absorbance of TMBCu2OAu nanocomposites system with various concentrations of H2O2 in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.0) at 45 °C for 30 min was measured from 500 to 800 nm using an UV/vis spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 6 , the absorbance at 652 nm was increased linearly with the increase of H2O2 concentration. Meanwhile, the linear detection range of H2O2 is 0.4–1000 μM (R2 = 0.998), which in general is wider than other nanomaterials [38,42–44] and particularly is significantly wider than the silver nanoparticles (i.e., 10–80 μM with R2 = 0.991) [45]. The detection limit was 0.13 μM through the formula: LOD = 3*σ/S (where σ denotes the blank standard deviation and S is the correction curve slope, respectively).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability experiment of Cu2OAu nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the catalytic stability of Cu2OAu nanocomposites, we conducted a stability experiment of different storage time gradients for the same sample. The results showed that the catalytic activity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites alcohol solution remained 96.8%, 91.5% and 86.0%, respectively, after being stored for 5, 8 and 11 months. This shows that Cu2OAu nanocomposites possess excellent catalytic stability, which is due to the fact that the AuNPs on the Cu2O surface can effectively inhibit the oxidative degeneration of Cu2O for maintaining high catalytic performance.

In general, we have used a simple two-step synthesis of Cu2OAu nanocomposites with low cost, small size, and large surface area. A series of experiments show that Cu2OAu nanocomposites exhibit excellent enzymatic activity due to synergistic effect between Cu2O and Au, which depends on the pH and temperature of the reaction system. In addition, the catalytic kinetics of Cu2OAu nanocomposites is consistent with that of Bell and the experimental results show that the affinity of Cu2OAu nanocomposites with the substrate is higher than that of HRP, indicating it has excellent catalytic ability. When quantitatively detecting hydrogen peroxide, because of their excellent enzyme-like catalytic enabling a linear detection range of 0.4–1000 μM with a detection limit of 0.13 μM. Obviously, the detection range is significantly larger than that exhibited by typical catalyst. In view of its significant catalytic properties, Cu2OAu may find extensive applications in bio-analytical chemistry and diagnostics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financed by the NSFC (21405029 and 51173035), Science and Technology Program of Zhejiang Province (LGF18H200005), Young Talent Development Project of Zhejiang Science and Technology Association (2016YCGC007), and the Social Development Project of Hangzhou (20160533B70).
